title: Google DeepMind ships Nano Banana 2 Lite and Gemini Omni Flash for cheap, fast media generation
subtitle: Nano Banana 2 Lite generates a 1K image in about four seconds at $0.034 per thousand — SynthID watermarks are on by default, and Omni Flash animates the stills into video.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Google DeepMind launched Nano Banana 2 Lite and Gemini Omni Flash this week for developers building generative media pipelines. Nano Banana 2 Lite is built on the Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite Image foundation and generates a text-to-image output in roughly four seconds at $0.034 per thousand images. Omni Flash is the companion video model — it accepts a Nano Banana 2 Lite still as a reference frame and animates it into a short high-quality clip. Both models embed SynthID watermarks in every output by default.

This is a deliberate tier below Nano Banana 2, released earlier this year. The Lite variant trades a small amount of quality for a large drop in latency and cost, which is the trade a designer running batch image generation cares about — hundreds or thousands of variants per campaign, not one hero image per session. Ranking fifth in the text-to-image arena at that price point puts Google in direct competition with FLUX, Ideogram, and Recraft for the high-volume programmatic slot.

The bigger story is where SynthID sits in the release. Google has embedded its watermark tech in every consumer-facing generative-media model it ships in 2026, ahead of the EU AI Act's transparency requirements that take effect August 2. When U.S. and EU regulators start requiring provenance signals on synthetic media — Colorado's AI Act, the EU's Code of Practice, and the U.K.'s AISI all point that direction — Google will already have a compliant answer. Its competitors mostly do not.

A takeaway for learners: cheap-and-fast generative media is now a commodity. The differentiators moving forward are provenance, licensing, and integration into a working creative pipeline — not the base image quality. If you are building on top of these APIs, invest early in tracking what you generated, what prompt produced it, and whether the watermark survives your downstream edits. That is the audit trail everything from client billing to platform policy will soon require.
